stem: A data set consists of the five values 6, 9, 12, 15, and 18. A sixth value is added to the data set so that the mean of the six values equals 13. What is the median of the six values?
explanation:
STEP A — Sort the original data set in ascending order: 6, 9, 12, 15, 18.

STEP B — Compute the original mean:
  Sum = 6 + 9 + 12 + 15 + 18 = 60
  Mean = 60 ÷ 5 = 12

STEP C — Identify the original median (n = 5, odd):
  Position = (5 + 1) ÷ 2 = 3rd value = 12

STEP D — Mode: no repeated values.

STEP E — Range = 18 − 6 = 12.

STEP F — Standard deviation: not involved.

STEP G — Find the sixth value needed to achieve a mean of 13:
  Required total sum for 6 values with mean 13: 13 × 6 = 78
  Sixth value = 78 − 60 = 18
  New data set (sorted): 6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 18

  New mean verification: (6 + 9 + 12 + 15 + 18 + 18) ÷ 6 = 78 ÷ 6 = 13 ✓

  New median (n = 6, even): average of the 3rd and 4th values
    Position of 3rd value: 12
    Position of 4th value: 15
    New median = (12 + 15) ÷ 2 = 13.5

STEP H — Correct answer = 13.5, which is choice C. ✓

STEP I — Distractor analysis:
  Choice A (12): MISCONCEPTION — median position error. The student identifies the correct median position formula for odd n (the 3rd value) but applies the old odd-n formula to the new even-n list. Taking the 3rd value of {6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 18} gives 12, not the correct average of the 3rd and 4th values.
  Choice B (13): MISCONCEPTION — statistic confusion. The student confuses the target mean (13) with the resulting median, treating the two statistics as equal. A student who does not complete the median computation step may default to the newly computed mean.
  Choice D (15): MISCONCEPTION — off-by-one median position error. The student uses the wrong median position for an even-n list, taking the 4th value (15) instead of averaging the 3rd and 4th values. This arises from applying the formula for the median position in an odd-n list [(n+1)/2 = 3.5, rounded up to 4] rather than the even-n procedure.

distractorRationale.A: Median position error: the student applies the odd-n median rule to the new 6-value list, taking the single middle (3rd) value = 12 instead of averaging the 3rd and 4th values.
distractorRationale.B: Statistic confusion: the student conflates the target mean (13) with the median of the new data set, stopping after computing the required mean without proceeding to find the median.
distractorRationale.C: Correct answer: the sixth value needed is 78 − 60 = 18, giving the sorted set {6, 9, 12, 15, 18, 18}; the median of this even-n set is (12 + 15) ÷ 2 = 13.5.
distractorRationale.D: Off-by-one median position error: the student rounds the even-n median position up to the 4th value (15) instead of averaging the 3rd and 4th values, misapplying the odd-n position formula.
